entries.intro: Something was scratching at the side of the house last night. Went out with a lantern and it was just a {wildlife_type}.
details: Heart was pounding for nothing. It looked at me like I was the one being unreasonable.
I stood there in my nightclothes feeling foolish while it wandered off.
reactions: The dark makes everything sound worse than it is.

entries.intro: I heard {mob_type} {direction} last night. Couldn't see anything, but the sound carries.
details: It's that low growling noise that makes your skin prickle even when you know they're far off.
I checked the locks twice before I went back to bed.
reactions: Probably nothing close. But I slept lighter after that.

entries.intro: Couldn't sleep, so I sat outside for a while. The stars were incredible last night.
details: You forget how many there are when you're always inside by dark. Just thousands of them, all at once.
It was cold enough to see my breath, but I didn't want to go in.
reactions: Sometimes the best part of a bad night is what you see when you give up on sleeping.

entries.intro: The wind was howling through the shutters all night. Sounded like someone trying to get in.
details: Every time I'd start to drift off, another gust would rattle the whole frame.
I finally stuffed a blanket into the gap. Didn't stop the noise, but it muffled it enough.
reactions: I'm exhausted today and I've got nothing to show for it except bags under my eyes.

statCheck.pass: The latch is worn down and doesn't seat properly. A new pin would fix it: ten minutes of work, tops.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they won't stay shut. The wind finds every weakness.

entries.intro: Heard footsteps on the gravel outside my window around midnight. Just someone heading to the privy, probably.
details: But they stopped right outside for a long moment. Just stood there.
By the time I got up to look, nobody was there.
reactions: I'm sure it was nothing. People walk at night. That's normal.

entries.intro: An owl's been nesting somewhere near the {poi_type}. You can hear it every night now.
details: It's a deep, steady sound. Almost soothing once you get used to it.
My neighbor hates it. Says it keeps her up. I think it's the one honest voice out there after dark.
reactions: I'd miss it if it left. The night feels emptier when it's quiet out there.

entries.intro: I woke up to complete silence last night. No wind, no insects, nothing.
details: You don't realize how much background sound there usually is until it all stops.
I lay there for a while just listening to my own heartbeat. Felt like the whole world was holding its breath.
reactions: It passed after a few minutes, but I didn't sleep well after that.

entries.intro: The moon was so bright last night I could see my shadow on the floor through the window.
details: Everything outside had this silver edge to it. The rooftops, the fences, even the mud puddles looked nice.
I almost went for a walk, but I thought better of it.
reactions: Moonlight makes everything look like a different place.

entries.intro: Took a guard shift last night. Mostly just me and the cold.
details: You'd think standing still in the dark would be boring, but your mind wanders to strange places.
Every shadow looks like something until you stare at it long enough.
reactions: I was glad when the sun came up. More glad than usual.

statCheck.pass: The trick is to stay moving. Walk a slow circuit. Keeps the blood warm and the eyes sharp.
statCheck.fail: I just stood there. Probably wasn't doing it right.

entries.intro: A {wildlife_type} ran across the path in front of me last night. Nearly jumped out of my skin.
details: It came out of nowhere, just a blur of movement and then gone.
I dropped my lantern and had to fumble around in the dark for it.
reactions: I laughed about it afterward. At the time I definitely did not laugh.

entries.intro: Someone left a door banging open at the {poi_type} last night. Woke half the street.
details: Just slamming back and forth in the wind for an hour before anyone went and fixed it.
You'd think the person responsible would've heard it too. Maybe they sleep heavier than the rest of us.
reactions: I'm not angry. I'm just tired.

entries.intro: There's a dog that howls every night around the same time. Every single night.
details: It starts low and builds up to this long, wavering note. Then silence. Then it goes again.
I've tried putting a pillow over my head, but it doesn't help. The owner claims it never howls: I think they're just too deep a sleeper to hear it.
reactions: I feel bad for the dog, honestly. It sounds lonely. But I also feel bad for me.

entries.intro: I've been waking up at the same hour every night this week. Right around the middle of the dark.
details: No noise, no bad dreams. Just suddenly wide awake, staring at the ceiling.
I get water, lie back down, and it takes forever to fall asleep again.
reactions: It's wearing me down. I worry about not sleeping, which makes it even harder to sleep.

entries.intro: Watched a {wildlife_type} from my window last night, just sitting in the moonlight like it owned the place.
details: It was perfectly still for the longest time. Then it turned its head, looked right at my window, and trotted off.
There's something about seeing an animal at night. They look like they belong there more than we do.
reactions: I liked it. Felt like I saw something I wasn't supposed to.

entries.intro: I heard something heavy moving around {direction} last night. Dragging sounds.
details: Couldn't tell what it was. Too dark to see, and I wasn't about to go investigate alone.
It went on for a while, then stopped. No idea what could make a sound like that.
reactions: I told myself it was probably someone moving firewood at a stupid hour. That's what I'm going with.

statCheck.pass: Sounded too heavy for an animal and too steady for something being blown around. My guess is someone hauling a cart.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what it was and I'd rather not find out.

entries.intro: The settlement looks different at night. Smaller, somehow.
details: All the spaces between buildings that feel normal during the day turn into these dark gaps you'd rather not look into.
Even the familiar paths feel longer when you can't see the end of them.
reactions: It's still home. It just doesn't feel like it in the dark.

entries.intro: Fireflies were out last night. Hundreds of them, all drifting through the grass.
details: My kid ran out to chase them before I could stop her. Came back with her hands cupped around one, grinning.
They only come out when it's warm and still. Feels like a gift when they do.
reactions: Some nights the dark gives you something beautiful instead of something to worry about.

entries.intro: I found fresh {mob_type} tracks near the fence this morning. They came through in the night.
details: The tracks went right along the perimeter and then back out toward the tree line.
Nothing was damaged, nothing taken. They were just passing through, I think.
reactions: Still doesn't sit right. That's closer than I'd like them getting.

statCheck.pass: Single set of tracks, moving fast. Probably a scout or a loner, not a raiding group.
statCheck.fail: Tracks are tracks. I don't know how to read much from them.

entries.intro: There's a creaking sound in my roof at night that I can't figure out. It's not the wind.
details: It happens at random intervals. Sometimes twice in a row, sometimes nothing for an hour.
I've checked the rafters three times and everything looks solid.
reactions: It's probably just the wood settling. But it sounds like something walking up there.

statCheck.pass: Timber joints shift when the temperature drops at night. The wood contracts unevenly and the pegs pop in their holes.
statCheck.fail: Houses make noises. I wish this one would stop.

entries.intro: Fell asleep outside by the fire last night. Woke up with dew on my face and a stiff back.
details: The embers were still going. Everything smelled like woodsmoke and wet grass.
There was a {wildlife_type} about ten paces away, just watching me. We looked at each other for a moment.
reactions: Worst sleep I've had in weeks, but the waking up part was oddly nice.

entries.intro: The night guard said he saw lights moving {direction}. Not torches: something fainter.
details: He pointed them out but by the time I looked, they were gone. Could've been anything.
He seemed rattled, though. And he's not the jumpy type.
reactions: Probably swamp gas or someone's lantern bobbing behind the trees. Probably.

statCheck.pass: Could be foxfire on rotting wood. It glows faintly in damp conditions and can look like it's moving if the light shifts.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what makes lights in the dark. Nothing good, I'd imagine.

entries.intro: My neighbor snores so loud I can hear it through the wall. Every night, without fail.
details: It's this deep, rumbling drone that you can feel in your chest if you're lying against the shared wall.
I've rearranged my bed twice trying to get away from it.
reactions: I can't even bring it up without sounding petty. But I'm losing sleep over it.

entries.intro: Woke up to rain hammering the roof in the middle of the night. Lay there and just listened.
details: There's something about rain at night when you're warm in bed. Makes you feel properly sheltered.
I almost felt guilty for enjoying it. Someone was probably out in it.
reactions: Best sleep I've had in ages, actually.

entries.intro: I keep my window cracked at night for the air. Last night I heard {mob_type} snarling somewhere out past the fields.
details: It was distant, but sound travels when everything else is quiet. That ugly, wet snarling noise.
I got up and closed the window. Didn't help: I could still hear it faintly through the walls.
reactions: I hate knowing they're out there while I'm trying to sleep.

entries.intro: The sunset last night was this deep orange that held for ages. Made the whole sky look like it was on fire.
details: Everyone just sort of stopped what they were doing and watched. Even the kids went quiet for a minute.
By the time it faded, the first stars were already out.
reactions: We don't get those very often. Felt like the day ending properly for once.

entries.intro: Took a walk after dark last night to clear my head. Bad idea.
details: Every noise made me flinch: a branch snapping, something rustling in the bushes. I turned around after five minutes and walked back twice as fast.
My heart was still going when I got home. From a walk. On a path I've taken a hundred times.
reactions: I don't know what I was thinking. Nighttime walks are for braver people than me.

entries.intro: The torches along the main path keep going out. Every morning at least two are dead.
details: We relight them and they're fine during the day. But overnight something snuffs them.
Could be the wind. Could be they're not sheltered enough. Either way, it leaves gaps in the light.
reactions: Nobody wants to walk through those dark patches. I've seen people take the long way around.

statCheck.pass: The oil's probably pooling unevenly in the cold. A longer wick would keep them burning through the temperature drop.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how torches work. I just know they should stay lit.

entries.intro: I dreamed about the {poi_type} last night. In the dream it was huge and I couldn't find the door.
details: I kept walking around the outside and every wall looked the same. Just the same boards over and over.
Then I heard someone inside laughing, and I woke up.
reactions: Dreams are nonsense. But I walked past the {poi_type} today and gave it a funny look anyway.

entries.intro: Thunder woke me last night and the lightning was right on top of us. You could feel it in your teeth.
details: The flash and the crack came at the same time: close enough that I was out of bed before I was awake.
I found a scorch mark on the big oak this morning. Hit it dead center.
reactions: I don't mind storms, but that one was personal.

entries.intro: I can always tell when someone's walking past my house at night. The floorboards outside creak in a specific order.
details: Left board, middle board, right board. Then footsteps on dirt.
Most nights it's the guard making rounds. But sometimes the pattern is different: slower, or stopping partway.
reactions: I've gotten too good at listening for things I probably shouldn't be tracking.

entries.intro: Stepped outside at dawn and the fog was so thick I couldn't see the fence three paces away.
details: Everything was muffled. Sounds came from weird directions. A rooster crowed and I couldn't tell which house it was from.
It burned off by mid-morning, but for a while there the settlement felt like it had shrunk to the size of my yard.
reactions: It's disorienting when you can't see a place you know by heart.

entries.intro: Night shift at the {poi_type} is the loneliest job in the settlement.
details: It's just you and the sounds for hours. The building talks to itself: settling, ticking, groaning in the cold.
You start having conversations in your head just to keep company.
reactions: I don't mind it most nights. But some nights it gets to you.

entries.intro: Something knocked over the water barrel outside my door last night. Made a tremendous crash.
details: I came out expecting trouble and found a soaking wet {wildlife_type} looking very confused about three feet away.
The barrel was on its side, water everywhere, and this animal just standing in the middle of the mess.
reactions: I yelled at it. It didn't care. I went back to bed.

entries.intro: I've noticed the {mob_type} are noisier after a new moon. When it's truly dark out there, they get restless.
details: You can hear them moving and calling to each other more than usual. Like the dark makes them bolder.
The guards say the same thing. Moonless nights are the tense ones.
reactions: I keep an eye on the moon cycle now. Never used to care about it before.

statCheck.pass: Makes sense. Their prey can't see them coming in the full dark, so they hunt more actively.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the dark changes them. It just does.

entries.intro: My cat brought something inside last night. Heard her crunching on it under the bed.
details: I didn't want to look. The sounds were enough to paint the picture.
She was so proud of herself, purring the whole time. Left the remains right by my pillow as a gift.
reactions: Good cat. Terrible housemate.

entries.intro: The crickets were so loud last night I thought they'd drive me mad. Just this wall of chirping from every direction.
details: You'd think you'd get used to it, but it's the kind of noise that sits right behind your eyes.
I actually got up and closed the window, which meant no breeze. So I was hot and annoyed instead of just annoyed.
reactions: Summer nights: can't sleep with the window open, can't sleep without it.

statCheck.pass: They chirp faster when it's warmer. If you count the chirps you can actually estimate the temperature. Useless knowledge at three in the morning.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they're so loud. I just know I want them to stop.

entries.intro: Sat on the porch last night and watched the bats come out. Dozens of them pouring out of the eaves.
details: They're fast. You only see them when they cross in front of something lighter: the sky, a torch, the moon.
They do this swooping thing where they fold and drop, then open up again. Like they're playing.
reactions: Ugly things up close, but at a distance in the dusk they're kind of graceful.

entries.intro: I left a candle burning in the window last night and woke up to find it had guttered out hours ago. House was pitch dark.
details: That moment when you wake up and can't see anything at all: takes your breath away for a second.
I had to feel my way across the room to find the tinderbox. Stubbed my toe on the chair in the process.
reactions: I should keep a spare candle closer to the bed.

entries.intro: There was a group of people talking outside late last night. Couldn't make out the words, just the murmur.
details: Voices at night carry differently. You hear the rhythm of conversation without any of the meaning.
They laughed once, all at the same time, and then it went quiet. Must've gone inside.
reactions: Made me feel oddly left out. Which is ridiculous: I was trying to sleep.

entries.intro: The guard dogs were barking at something {direction} for nearly an hour last night.
details: Started with one, then they all joined in. That frantic, high-pitched barking that means they see something.
Nobody found anything when they checked. The dogs calmed down eventually but they were twitchy the rest of the night.
reactions: When the dogs are upset, I'm upset. They know things we don't.

statCheck.pass: Dogs will react to {mob_type} scent on the wind even when the creatures are nowhere close. The smell alone is enough.
statCheck.fail: Could've been anything. I trust the dogs' noses more than my guesses.

entries.intro: I've started keeping a lantern lit all night. Costs me in oil, but I sleep better with a little glow.
details: The shadows still move when the flame flickers, which isn't ideal. But it's better than total dark.
I used to think people who needed a light at night were being childish. My partner complains the glow keeps them up, so now one of us is always awake anyway.
reactions: Sleep is sleep. I'll pay whatever it costs to get some.

entries.intro: Full moon last night and the whole settlement was lit up like early morning. You could read a sign from fifty paces.
details: The shadows were sharp and deep. Every post and rail threw a line of black on the ground.
Someone's chickens were confused and started clucking like it was dawn.
reactions: I prefer the bright nights. Everything you can see is a thing you don't have to imagine.

entries.intro: Heard a {wildlife_type} calling out in the dark last night. Went on and on, this long repeating cry.
details: Sounded lonesome. Like it was looking for another one and not finding it.
After a while a second one answered from further off, and the first one went quiet.
reactions: There was something relieving about the answer coming. Even though it's just animals.

entries.intro: I accidentally slammed my door going out for water last night. Woke up at least two neighbors.
details: I saw candles light up in two windows and felt absolutely terrible.
Tried to creep back inside but the hinges squealed anyway.
reactions: I owe them an apology. And possibly some quiet hinges.

entries.intro: The night sky was clear enough to see the band of stars that runs across the middle. Haven't seen it that bright in months.
details: My grandmother used to call it the river. Said the dead sail along it to wherever they're going.
I don't know about all that, but it's something to look at when sleep won't come.
reactions: Makes you feel small, but not in a bad way.

entries.intro: Something got into the chicken coop overnight. Lost two birds.
details: Feathers everywhere this morning, and a hole chewed through the back corner.
The rest of the chickens are spooked. Won't come out, won't eat, just huddled in the corner.
reactions: I worked hard to build that coop right. Apparently not right enough.

statCheck.pass: The teeth marks on the wood are small and sharp: probably a weasel or a marten. They can squeeze through a gap you wouldn't believe.
statCheck.fail: Something with teeth and determination. That's all I can tell.

entries.intro: The frost was thick enough this morning that everything crunched underfoot. Must've dropped hard after midnight.
details: The rooftops were white and the grass looked like it was made of glass.
Every spider web between the fences was outlined in ice crystals. Actually beautiful, if you weren't freezing.
reactions: Cold nights make for pretty mornings, at least.

entries.intro: I keep hearing this tapping at my window at night. Regular as a heartbeat.
details: First few times I jumped up to check. Nothing. Just the dark and the glass.
I finally figured out it's a branch from the vine that grew up the wall. Hits the glass whenever there's the slightest breeze.
reactions: I should cut that vine back. But now that I know what it is, it's almost comforting.

entries.intro: Pulled guard duty near the {poi_type} last night and heard {mob_type} moving in the brush beyond the perimeter.
details: They weren't coming closer, just parallel to the fence line. You could track them by the sound.
I kept my torch up and my back to the wall. They moved on after a while.
reactions: My shift ended two hours later and it felt like ten.

statCheck.pass: They were skirting the firelight. {mob_type} avoid open flame when they can, especially torches. The perimeter's working.
statCheck.fail: They were close. That's all I know and that's enough.

entries.intro: I heard singing last night, very faint, coming from somewhere {direction}. Couldn't place it.
details: It wasn't a tune I recognized. Just a voice, rising and falling, almost like humming.
When I opened the window wider to hear better, it stopped.
reactions: Probably someone who couldn't sleep. The night carries voices further than people think.

entries.intro: My roof leaks in one spot and last night the dripping started around midnight. Right over my head.
details: I put a pot under it and then had to listen to that plinking sound all night instead.
The spot on the ceiling has a brown ring around it now. It's been growing all season.
reactions: I'll patch it tomorrow. I've been saying that for a week.

entries.intro: The best thing about warm nights is leaving the door open and feeling the air move through the house.
details: You get the smell of the fields and the sound of the night insects. It's like sleeping outside without the bugs.
Woke up with a {wildlife_type} sitting in my doorway this morning, bold as anything.
reactions: Maybe I'll start closing the door again.

entries.intro: The guard on the east side fell asleep on his shift last night. Someone found him propped against the post, snoring.
details: I can't really blame him. Those overnight shifts are brutal, especially the cold ones.
But it means there was a gap in the watch for who knows how long.
reactions: We need more people on rotation. The ones we have are stretched too thin.

entries.intro: I swear the shadows move differently on cloudy nights. When there's no moon, everything shifts.
details: With moonlight, shadows stay put. You know what's casting them. Without it, every dark patch feels alive.
I caught myself staring at a fence post for a full minute last night, convinced it had moved.
reactions: It's just your eyes trying to make sense of nothing. Doesn't make it less unsettling.

statCheck.pass: Your eyes switch to a different kind of vision in low light. It's blurrier, catches movement better, but invents motion where there isn't any.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the dark plays tricks. It just does.

entries.intro: Woke up before dawn and made tea in the dark. Sat on the step and watched the sky change color.
details: It goes from black to deep blue to this thin line of gold at the edge, so slowly you almost don't notice.
The birds start before the light does. First one, then a few more, then suddenly all of them.
reactions: I should do that more often. There's a whole part of the day most people just sleep through.

entries.intro: Heard a crash from the {poi_type} last night. Turned out a shelf collapsed under its own weight.
details: Nobody was hurt, but the noise in the dark was enough to send half the street running over.
We all stood there in our nightclothes looking at a pile of broken wood and feeling foolish.
reactions: Nice to know people show up when there's trouble. Even if the trouble was just gravity.

entries.intro: The temperature dropped so fast last night I could hear the timbers in the walls popping and cracking.
details: Every few minutes: crack. Like someone snapping a stick inside the wall.
I know it's just the wood contracting. But when you're half asleep, your brain doesn't care about explanations.
reactions: I'd rather have the cold than the heat, but I could do without the sound effects.

statCheck.pass: Green timber does that more than seasoned wood. Whoever built this place used fresh-cut lumber and it's been complaining about it ever since.
statCheck.fail: Wood cracks when it's cold. That's the extent of what I know about it.

entries.intro: A shooting star went right across the sky last night. Bright enough to light up the yard for a second.
details: I almost missed it. Happened to be looking up at the right moment.
It left this faint green trail that faded before I could even point at it.
reactions: You never see those when other people are around. It's always just you.

entries.intro: I left laundry on the line overnight and something dragged half of it into the yard.
details: Found a shirt on the ground with muddy paw prints on it. Looked like a {wildlife_type} used it as a bed.
Another piece was just gone entirely. No idea where it ended up.
reactions: I'm more confused than angry. What does a {wildlife_type} want with my laundry?

entries.intro: The frogs down by the water start up every night the moment the sun goes down. The noise is incredible.
details: It's hundreds of them, all going at once. This pulsing, throbbing chorus that fills the whole valley.
Some nights it fades into the background. Other nights it's all you can hear.
reactions: My partner says it's soothing. I say it's relentless.

entries.intro: Found muddy boot prints on my porch this morning. Someone was standing right outside my door in the night.
details: Two prints, side by side, facing the door. Like they stood there and then left.
Could've been anyone checking on something. Could've been the guard. But nobody mentioned it.
reactions: I'm not jumping to conclusions. But I am going to start latching the door.

statCheck.pass: The tread pattern is the guard's standard issue boots. Probably just doing a door check and moved on.
statCheck.fail: Boots are boots. I can't tell one pair from another.

entries.intro: There's always that one moment at night when the last light goes out in the settlement and it's truly dark.
details: You can feel it happen even from inside. The quality of the dark changes through the walls.
After that, every sound outside stands out sharper. You notice things you'd normally sleep right through.
reactions: I don't love that moment. It feels like a door closing.

entries.intro: Heard two {wildlife_type} fighting outside my window at some ungodly hour. Screeching and thumping.
details: By the time I got to the window they'd moved on, but you could hear them carrying the argument down the lane.
Found tufts of fur on the ground this morning. Someone lost that fight.
reactions: Animals have their own dramas. They just stage them at midnight.

entries.intro: The wind changed direction around midnight last night. Brought a smell with it: earthy, damp, almost sweet.
details: It was coming from {direction}. Smelled like wet leaves and turned soil.
Pleasant at first, but it had this undercurrent to it. Like something rotting under the sweet part.
reactions: Probably just the marsh. But at night your nose tells you stories the same way your eyes do.

statCheck.pass: That's decomposing vegetation from the low ground. Wind rarely comes from that direction unless the pressure is dropping: rain by tomorrow, probably.
statCheck.fail: Smelled wrong. That's all I've got.

entries.intro: I made the mistake of having tea too late last night. Was awake until the early hours.
details: Tried lying down, tried reading, tried counting my breaths. Nothing worked.
Ended up reorganizing my pantry shelf by lantern light just to have something to do.
reactions: At least the pantry looks good. The rest of me does not.

entries.intro: On clear nights you can see torchlight from the next settlement over. Just this little warm dot on the horizon.
details: It flickers in and out behind the trees. Some nights it's steady, some nights it's just a glimmer.
There are people over there going about their own business, completely unaware I'm watching their light.
reactions: It's comforting to know someone else is awake out there.

entries.intro: A moth the size of my palm got into the house last night. Went straight for the lantern and wouldn't leave it alone.
details: It was throwing itself at the glass over and over. This soft, insistent thudding.
I tried to shoo it out but it kept circling back. Eventually I just put the lantern outside and let it have what it wanted.
reactions: Then I was in the dark. Which I suppose was the moth's plan all along.

entries.intro: I can hear the river from my bed if the night is quiet enough. Just barely.
details: It's this low, constant shushing sound at the very edge of hearing. Most people probably wouldn't notice it.
On windy nights it disappears completely. I miss it when it's gone.
reactions: It's the most reliable sound in the settlement. Everything else stops at some point: the river never does.

entries.intro: The outhouse door doesn't latch properly and it bangs in the wind all night. I've mentioned it three times.
details: It's not loud enough to justify getting up about, but it's too regular to ignore.
Bang. Pause. Bang. Pause. For hours.
reactions: I'm going to fix it myself tomorrow. A tiny problem that ruins a whole night's sleep feels like the biggest problem in the world.

entries.intro: Watched a storm roll in from {direction} last night. You could see the lightning in the clouds long before the rain hit.
details: The sky would light up for a second and you'd see the outlines of everything: the hills, the trees, the rooftops. Then dark again.
When the thunder finally reached us it was this deep, rolling rumble that went on and on.
reactions: I watched the whole thing from the porch. Felt like a show put on just for me.

entries.intro: There's a cat that sits on the wall behind the {poi_type} every night. Never moves, never makes a sound.
details: Its eyes catch the torchlight and glow this eerie green. You'd swear it was a statue if it didn't blink.
I've tried to pet it during the day and it won't come near me. But at night it sits there like a sentry.
reactions: I've decided it's good luck. That's easier than being unnerved by it.

entries.intro: Night guard found {mob_type} scratches on the outer fence posts this morning. Fresh ones.
details: Deep gouges, about chest height. Whatever made them was big enough to reach the top rail.
The posts held, but a few more nights of that and they won't.
reactions: We need to reinforce that section. Sooner rather than later.

statCheck.pass: The claw spacing suggests a single large one, not a group. Territorial marking, most likely: they scratch to claim boundaries.
statCheck.fail: Big scratches, big creature. That's as much as I can figure.

entries.intro: I love the smell of woodsmoke at night. Someone always has their fire going late.
details: You step outside and the air has that warm, dry edge to it. Mixes with the cold in this perfect way.
You can trace the smoke trail up if the moon's bright enough. It just curls and dissolves.
reactions: It's the smell of people being home and safe. Hard to be anxious when you're breathing that in.

entries.intro: Had a nightmare about the {poi_type} being on fire. Woke up smelling smoke and panicked.
details: Ran outside barefoot before I was properly awake. Everything was fine: it was just someone's chimney.
Stood there with my heart hammering while two people stared at me from across the lane.
reactions: Dreams shouldn't follow you out of bed like that.

entries.intro: There's a stretch of fence where the torchlight doesn't quite reach. I walk past it fast every time.
details: It's maybe ten paces of shadow between two pools of light. Nothing's ever been there.
I've seen other people speed up through that stretch too. Nobody talks about it, but we all do it.
reactions: Grown adults walking fast through a shadow. That's what the dark does to people.

entries.intro: Last night was the first warm enough to sleep with the window full open. Felt like a turning point.
details: The night air was soft and you could smell the blossoms from the trees along the fence.
Fell asleep to crickets and woke up to birds. That's the best kind of night.
reactions: Winter's finally letting go. About time.

entries.intro: Someone's rooster doesn't know what time it is. Crowed at midnight, then again at two, then again at four.
details: By the time it actually crowed at dawn I was already awake and angry about it.
Half the settlement was up because of that bird. You could hear people grumbling from their windows.
reactions: That rooster has made more enemies in one night than most people make in a lifetime.

statCheck.pass: Roosters crow at any bright light, not just dawn. A torch being carried past the coop at the wrong angle would set it off every time.
statCheck.fail: The bird's broken. That's my diagnosis.

entries.intro: I was on watch last night and a {wildlife_type} walked right through the gate like it belonged here.
details: Just strolled past me, calm as anything. Sniffed around the water trough and then walked back out.
I stood there with my torch raised feeling like I should've done something. But what do you do?
reactions: At least it wasn't something with teeth.

statCheck.pass: They've learned the settlement means easy water and food scraps. We're part of their territory now, in a way.
statCheck.fail: Animals go where they want. We just happen to be in the way.

entries.intro: The well chain rattles in the wind at night. Sounds exactly like someone drawing water when no one's there.
details: The first time I heard it I went out to check who was at the well past midnight. Nobody.
Now I know what it is, but my stomach still tightens when I hear it.
reactions: I've thought about tying the chain down. But then I'd have to untie it every morning.

entries.intro: I overslept this morning because I was up half the night listening to {mob_type} {direction}.
details: They were making that racket they make when they're fighting over something. Went on for ages.
Eventually it stopped and I must've passed out from exhaustion, because the next thing I knew it was bright outside.
reactions: Lost half my morning and all of my patience.

entries.intro: The dew was so heavy this morning that every spider web in the settlement was visible. Hundreds of them.
details: Every fence rail, every post, every gap in the woodwork. All threaded with these perfect little webs, all glittering.
They'd been there all along, but you only see them when the conditions are right.
reactions: Makes you wonder what else is there that you just can't see yet.

entries.intro: I keep a knife by the bed now. Started after I heard {mob_type} close to the walls last month.
details: I've never had to use it. Probably never will. But my hand reaches for it every time I hear something.
My partner thinks I'm overreacting. Maybe I am.
reactions: I'd rather have it and not need it than the other way around.

entries.intro: There's a gap under my door that lets in a cold draft all night. I can feel it from the bed.
details: I stuffed rags in there but they shift around and it comes back.
Some mornings there's actually a line of frost on the floor where the draft comes through.
reactions: It's on the list of things to fix. Every night I tell myself I'll deal with it tomorrow, and every morning I forget.

entries.intro: Shared a watch shift with the old guard last night. He barely talked, just stood there scanning the dark.
details: He's been doing it for years. Knows every sound, every shadow, every rhythm of the night out here.
At one point he held up his hand and whispered 'there' without even looking. Sure enough, a {wildlife_type} crossed the path a moment later.
reactions: I want to be that calm about it. I'm not there yet.

entries.intro: The stars were so clear last night I tried to count them. Got to forty before I gave up.
details: Every time I'd focus on one area, I'd notice more in my peripheral vision. They just kept going.
My neck hurt from looking up. Lay down in the grass and that was better but colder.
reactions: There's always more than you think. That's the thing about stars.

entries.intro: Something was digging near the {poi_type} overnight. Found fresh holes this morning.
details: Three holes, all the same size, along the foundation. Shallow, like something was rooting for grubs.
Dirt scattered everywhere. Whoever has to clean that up is going to be in a mood.
reactions: It's the kind of thing that's harmless but feels like a violation.

statCheck.pass: Looks like a badger or something similar. They dig for beetles in soft ground near foundations where the soil stays warm.
statCheck.fail: Something was hungry enough to dig for it. That's all I've got.

entries.intro: I burned my hand on the lantern last night trying to relight it in a hurry. Heard something outside and panicked.
details: Fumbled with the tinderbox, caught the wrong part of the frame, and now I've got a blister across two fingers.
The thing outside turned out to be a branch falling. Just a branch.
reactions: My hand hurts and my dignity hurts worse.

entries.intro: I don't mind the dark itself. It's the sounds in the dark that get to me.
details: During the day you hear a noise and you look at what made it. At night you hear a noise and you imagine what made it.
A scratching sound at noon is a branch. The same sound at midnight is something trying to get in.
reactions: I'd sleep better if I was deaf, honestly.

entries.intro: We had a bonfire last night. Half the settlement came out and we sat around it until well past dark.
details: The fire pops and sends sparks up into the black sky and everyone watches them go. Like tiny stars falling upward.
People talk more honestly by firelight, I think. Something about the dark around you makes you focus on whoever's in the glow.
reactions: Those are the good nights. Makes you remember why you live where people are.

entries.intro: A bat flew into my house through the open window last night. Absolute chaos.
details: It was bouncing off the walls, knocking things over, and I was ducking and swinging a broom like a fool.
Took me fifteen minutes to herd it back out the window. The whole time it was screaming. I was also screaming.
reactions: My voice is still hoarse and my kitchen is a wreck.

entries.intro: Last night was so still you could hear the torch flames hissing from twenty paces away.
details: No wind, no insects, just this soft, steady sizzle from the oil burning.
My own footsteps sounded too loud. I kept looking down like the ground itself was complaining.
reactions: Quiet like that should be peaceful. Instead it just makes you more aware of every little thing.

entries.intro: I watched the clouds swallow the moon last night. Like something eating it from the edge.
details: One moment the yard was bright, the next it was gone. Just swallowed up.
The dark felt heavier after that. Like the moon had been holding something back.
reactions: I went inside and didn't come back out.

statCheck.pass: Weather front moving in from the coast. When clouds eat the moon that fast, it usually means heavy cover by morning.
statCheck.fail: Clouds came, moon went. That's all I saw.

entries.intro: There was a fight between two people outside the {poi_type} last night. Shouting and carrying on.
details: Couldn't make out what it was about. Just raised voices, that sharp tone people get when they're past the point of caring who hears.
It ended with a door slamming and then silence. The whole street was probably lying awake pretending they didn't hear.
reactions: None of my business. But I lost twenty minutes of sleep to other people's problems.

entries.intro: I found a {wildlife_type} sleeping under my porch this morning. Must've been there all night.
details: It had made a little nest of leaves and just curled up in there. Didn't even wake up when I walked over it.
I left some water out and went about my day. It was gone by noon.
reactions: Can't blame it for wanting a roof. We all want the same thing at night.

entries.intro: The watch bell rang once last night. Just once, then nothing.
details: One ring means 'alert': someone saw something. But nobody called out and the second ring never came.
I lay there for an hour waiting for the follow-up. It never came.
reactions: Probably a false alarm. But that's a long hour when you're waiting for the second bell.

statCheck.pass: Single ring with no follow-up usually means the guard spotted movement but couldn't confirm it. Standard procedure is to hold and observe.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the bell codes well enough. One ring could mean a lot of things.

entries.intro: There's something about the hour just before dawn. The sky isn't dark anymore but it's not light either.
details: Everything is this flat grey-blue. No shadows, no color. Like the world hasn't decided what it is yet.
The air is always coldest right then. One last bite before the sun takes over.
reactions: It's a lonely hour. But there's a promise in it.

entries.intro: I heard {mob_type} howling last night. Not close, but not far enough.
details: That sound goes right through the walls. Right through the blankets. Right into your bones.
I lay there rigid until it stopped, and even then I couldn't relax for another hour.
reactions: There's no getting used to that sound. I don't care what anyone says.

statCheck.pass: Howling usually means they're calling to regroup, not hunt. If they were close and hunting, you wouldn't hear them at all.
statCheck.fail: All I know is I don't like it. The why doesn't make it easier to hear.

entries.intro: Woke up to the smell of bread baking. Someone at the {poi_type} starts before dawn every day.
details: It drifts through the whole settlement while it's still dark out. This warm, yeasty smell that doesn't belong to nighttime.
I lay there half awake just breathing it in. Felt like the morning reaching back to get me.
reactions: That might be my favorite smell in the world. Especially at that hour.

entries.intro: The settlement gate squeaks every time the guard opens it for patrol. I can track their whole route by the sound.
details: Squeak out. Footsteps on gravel. Long pause. Footsteps back. Squeak in.
It happens about every hour, all night. Like a clock made of hinges and boots.
reactions: Someone should oil that gate. But honestly, I'd miss the routine.

statCheck.pass: A squeaky gate is almost a feature. You can't open it quietly, which means nobody sneaks through unheard.
statCheck.fail: I never thought about it that way. I just thought it needed oil.

entries.intro: I lay awake last night thinking about things that don't bother me during the day. The dark makes worries bigger.
details: Stuff that seems manageable in the morning turns into this looming weight at two in the morning.
I know it's not real. I know the problems are the same size they were at noon. But my brain doesn't care.
reactions: The best thing about morning is that everything shrinks back to its actual size.
